romania.title: 😂 Studiu: dacă renunți la accentul de ardelean îți crește IQ-ul cu 40 de puncte
romania.summary: Un studiu publicat de Institutul Național de Cercetări Cognitive din Cluj relevă o corelație puternică între abandonarea accentului ardelenesc și creșterea semnificativă a coeficientului de inteligență. Potrivit cercetătorilor, renunțarea la particularitățile fonetice specifice zonei Transilvaniei poate determina o creștere medie de 40 de puncte a IQ-ului, efect observat în special la subiecții cu expunere prelungită […] The post Studiu: dacă renunți la accentul de ardelean îți crește IQ-ul cu 40 de puncte appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.title: Pentru Fundația Motivation, duminica trecută a fost despre fapte bune și, mai ales, despre o echipă unită în jurul unui scop dedicat binelui.
romania.summary: #TeamMotivation a alergat la Semimaraton Galați ca să schimbe în bine viețile copiilor și adulților cu dizabilități care au nevoie de sprijin în încercarea lor de a-și recăpăta independența. Evenimentul organizat de Fundația Comunitară Galați a adunat ambasadori a zeci de cauze pentru care alergătorii participanți au făcut tot posibilul să ajungă la finish. Pe lângă emoțiile și încurajările care au condimentat cursele din cadrul maratonului, pentru Fundația Motivation evenimentul a arătat așa: 14 utilizatori de scaun rulant... The post Pentru Fundația Motivation, duminica trecută a fost despre fapte bune și, mai ales, despre o echipă unită în jurul unui scop dedicat binelui. appeared first on Fundatia Motivation Romania .

romania.summary: Zbor Hub Brașov a găzduit finala națională Arena Urșilor Junior 2026, o competiție dedicată liceenilor cu idei de afaceri curajoase și planuri antreprenoriale inovatoare. Brașovul a devenit, pentru câteva ore, … Read More The post Brașovul a devenit capitala tinerilor antreprenori din România la Arena Urșilor Junior 2026 appeared first on Stiri pozitive .

medical.title: Mediterranean diet is associated with better psychological well-being, study suggests
medical.summary: Following a Mediterranean diet may provide additional benefits for mental health. A new study has now linked it to greater psychological well-being among people over 50. The research is the result of a collaboration between University College London and the Barcelona Institute for Global Health (ISGlobal), a center supported by the "la Caixa" Foundation, and has been published in BMJ Open.

medical.title: Blood test may help identify which colorectal cancer patients most likely to benefit from chemotherapy after surgery
medical.summary: A blood test may help identify which patients with colorectal cancer that has spread to the liver are most likely to benefit from chemotherapy after surgery, according to research presented today at the ESMO Gastrointestinal Cancers Congress 2026.

medical.title: Study reveals how the uterine microbiome may impact pregnancy success
medical.summary: Researchers from Trinity College Dublin have uncovered new evidence that the communities of bacteria living in the uterus may play an important role in determining whether pregnancy is successful following assisted reproductive technologies (ART) such as IVF. Among the key findings was one notable surprise, which suggests the biological signals that have long been used to govern the timing of embryo transfer may be misleading.

medical.title: New brush test detects oral cancer in one hour
medical.summary: A paper published in the journal Biomarker Research by a cross-university team led by Queen Mary University of London researchers validates the use of a noninvasive brush biopsy test that can detect oral cancer within one hour.

medical.title: Exposure to everyday chemicals can add up: A toxicologist offers simple steps to reduce your dose
medical.summary: Imagine an ordinary Tuesday. You wash your hair, put on deodorant, drink coffee, pack lunch in a plastic container and commute through traffic to get to work. At work, the custodial staff wipes down a shared table with disinfectant. At home, you cook dinner, clean the kitchen and run the dishwasher.

medical.title: New AI system provides treatment recommendations for complex blood cancers
medical.summary: Researchers from the German Cancer Research Center (DKFZ), the HI-STEM* Stem Cell Institute and Heidelberg University Hospital (UKHD) have developed "HemaGuide," an AI assistant that supports physicians in making difficult treatment decisions. In extensive testing, the system has shown a high degree of agreement with the recommendations of experienced experts and could significantly improve access to specialized and personalized cancer care.

medical.title: New virus catalog reveals which pathogens pose the greatest threat
medical.summary: In a typical year, scientists discover two or three viruses that have never been seen in people before. The number fluctuates, but the trend has been fairly steady since the 1960s.

medical.title: The myth of the 'lizard brain' and the real trade-off inside your mind
medical.summary: So many of life's most pivotal decisions come down to one question: Should you listen to your logic or your emotions? Popular culture often frames this tension as a struggle between two minds—a "more evolved" rational layer built atop an ancient "lizard brain" driven by primal instincts. This battle of the brains has also been playing out over the course of evolution, but not as a simple clash between old and new.

medical.title: AI test predicts breast cancer recurrence in hours, not weeks, across 3,500 patients
medical.summary: In recent years, notable advances have been made in diagnosing and treating breast cancer. However, its recurrence continues to plague thousands, deepening the need to find ways to better predict the likelihood of its return.

medical.title: A surprising brain discovery is forcing scientists to rethink movement disorders
medical.summary: A surprising discovery is overturning a long-held assumption about how the brain’s movement center works. Researchers found that two key cerebellar cell types—thought to be tightly linked—often don’t behave in predictable ways, even though one directly influences the other. The finding suggests scientists may have been relying on the wrong signals when studying disorders such as dystonia, ataxia, and tremor.

medical.title: Modern neuroscience is rediscovering an idea Freud had 130 years ago
medical.summary: What if Sigmund Freud was onto something that modern neuroscience is only now beginning to explain? A new paper argues that today's leading theory of the brain—as a prediction machine constantly anticipating the world—closely mirrors ideas psychoanalysis has explored for more than a century.

medical.title: Scientists discover a surprising link between vitamin C and brain health
medical.summary: Could something as simple as vitamin C help support a healthier aging brain? In a study of more than 2,000 older adults in Japan, researchers found that people with lower vitamin C levels in their blood also tended to have less gray matter and weaker connections in a key brain network involved in memory, attention, and other cognitive functions.

medical.title: Melanoma's secret to cheating death has finally been revealed
medical.summary: Scientists have solved a long-standing mystery by discovering the missing genetic ingredient that helps melanoma cells become effectively immortal. The breakthrough could open the door to new treatments aimed at disrupting one of cancer's most important survival strategies.

medical.title: Scientists discover a completely different way to fight viruses
medical.summary: Researchers have uncovered an unexpected antiviral defense system in sea anemones that works very differently from the one humans use. The discovery suggests evolution developed multiple ways to combat viruses, challenging long-held ideas about how animal immune systems evolved.

medical.title: One injection reversed osteoarthritis in weeks
medical.summary: A Colorado research team has created experimental osteoarthritis treatments that appear to regenerate damaged joints rather than just relieve pain. In animal studies, a single injection restored arthritic joints to a healthy state within weeks, while a second therapy repaired cartilage and bone defects by harnessing the body’s own healing cells.

science.title: High levels of forever chemicals found on wastewater filters
science.summary: As cities across the nation and the globe increasingly turn to advanced water purification systems to expand their drinking water supplies, researchers at Texas A&M University have identified a critical environmental safety consideration. A new study from the Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering shows that per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS), commonly known as "forever chemicals," accumulate significantly on the purification filters used in advanced treatment to recycle wastewater into drinking water.

science.title: Did elephant energetics decide Hannibal's Alpine crossing route?
science.summary: A new analysis sheds light on the most likely route for the Carthaginian general's famous crossing of the Alps. The study, led by the University of Oxford and iDiv/Friedrich Schiller University Jena, reveals that the Col de la Traversette would have been the least energy-intensive route. The findings have been published in Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences.

science.title: Cave finds reveal modern humans and Neanderthals may have shared long-term cultural continuity
science.summary: Tens of thousands of years ago, Homo sapiens coexisted with Neanderthals, Homo neanderthalensis. Many of us living today carry a small amount of Neanderthal DNA, indicating that the two species may have shared much more than just the same land. Now, a breakthrough archaeological discovery has revealed that the two species did not merely cross paths: they possibly shared a common culture that spanned more than 20,000 years.

science.title: Physics-informed AI could accelerate development of controlled-release drug patches, bandages
science.summary: Brown University researchers have developed a new artificial intelligence method for predicting the rate at which materials used in controlled drug-release systems release therapeutic agents. The new method could slash the development time for new therapeutic patches, bandages and implants.

science.title: Bees reveal emotion-like reactions, from 'lip licking' to head shaking, in new videos
science.summary: New research proving bumblebees exhibit emotion-like behaviors—previously thought to exist only in mammalian species—has implications for how scientists understand the consciousness of insects.

science.title: AI could hurt employers in race for top talent
science.summary: Companies embracing AI to recruit faster could be damaging their ability to compete for top talent, according to a major new study.

science.title: Neighbors shape plant life more than expected, scientists find
science.summary: Scientists at Leiden University have discovered that plants are strongly influenced by their neighbors, not just above ground but also through hidden networks in the soil. Their findings challenge long-held ideas about how plants shape their environment and could help improve sustainable farming.

science.title: NASA Takes Flight For America’s 250th
science.summary: NASA Administrator Jared Isaacman leads a flyover featuring his personally owned Northrop F-5 Tiger during the Great American State Fair on July 4, 2026, on the National Mall in Washington, D.C. For 250 years, America has pushed the boundaries of what’s possible. From the earliest days of exploration, to the first steps on the Moon […]

science.title: First synthetic protein motor moves along DNA in controlled, programmable steps
science.summary: Researchers from UNSW Sydney have built the first artificial protein motor capable of taking controlled, directional steps along a DNA track. The protein, dubbed Tumbleweed, moves by alternating between three "feet" that bind to specific DNA sequences. By changing the surrounding chemical environment, the researchers can control both when the motor steps and the direction it travels.

science.title: Algorithm determines which firms have advanced capabilities—and what others must build first
science.summary: Understanding how firms build their capabilities, such as managerial skills, technology adoption and innovation capacity, is important for explaining why some firms perform better and how others can improve. However, unlike sales or profits, firm capabilities are not easily quantifiable. So existing studies have used surveys or interviews to assess them, but these methods are costly, time-consuming and susceptible to bias.

science.title: Four new groups of indigenous cacao varieties discovered in Peru
science.summary: A new genetic analysis of hundreds of cacao trees representing traditional Amazonian varieties grown on farms across Peru has revealed four previously unidentified, genetically distinct groups. Lambert Motilal, with colleagues from the Cocoa Research Center, The University of the West Indies, and Martha S. Calderon and Danilo E. Bustamante, with their colleagues from the Universidad Nacional Toribio Rodríguez de Mendoza, partnered to explore Peru's untapped cacao diversity.

science.title: Unexpected discovery yields new graphene oxide production method
science.summary: Researchers in the Texas A&M University J. Mike Walker '66 Department of Mechanical Engineering have developed a new method for producing graphene oxide, a high-value carbon nanomaterial used in batteries, electronics and advanced manufacturing.

science.title: What do people really eat? New global database gives best answer yet
science.summary: A major new resource that provides one of the most comprehensive pictures yet of what people are eating around the world has been introduced in a new study by a UCL and University of Oxford researcher.

science.title: Paleontologists make 'one in a million' discovery of soft tissue preserved in 450-million-year-old fossil
science.summary: Before the oldest dinosaur, before animals or even plants had expanded onto dry land, ancient relatives of starfish called crinoids, resembling stalked sea flowers, were among the first creatures to flourish in Earth's earliest coral reefs more than 450 million years ago. The study of fossilized crinoids helps scientists understand how these animals evolved and offers rare insight into the origins of complex life.

environment.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
environment.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

environment.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
environment.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

environment.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
environment.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

environment.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
environment.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

environment.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
environment.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

environment.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
environment.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

environment.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
environment.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

environment.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
environment.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

mix_items.title: Pentru Fundația Motivation, duminica trecută a fost despre fapte bune și, mai ales, despre o echipă unită în jurul unui scop dedicat binelui.
mix_items.summary: #TeamMotivation a alergat la Semimaraton Galați ca să schimbe în bine viețile copiilor și adulților cu dizabilități care au nevoie de sprijin în încercarea lor de a-și recăpăta independența. Evenimentul organizat de Fundația Comunitară Galați a adunat ambasadori a zeci de cauze pentru care alergătorii participanți au făcut tot posibilul să ajungă la finish. Pe lângă emoțiile și încurajările care au condimentat cursele din cadrul maratonului, pentru Fundația Motivation evenimentul a arătat așa: 14 utilizatori de scaun rulant... The post Pentru Fundația Motivation, duminica trecută a fost despre fapte bune și, mai ales, despre o echipă unită în jurul unui scop dedicat binelui. appeared first on Fundatia Motivation Romania .

mix_items.title: Brașovul a devenit capitala tinerilor antreprenori din România la Arena Urșilor Junior 2026
mix_items.summary: Zbor Hub Brașov a găzduit finala națională Arena Urșilor Junior 2026, o competiție dedicată liceenilor cu idei de afaceri curajoase și planuri antreprenoriale inovatoare. Brașovul a devenit, pentru câteva ore, … Read More The post Brașovul a devenit capitala tinerilor antreprenori din România la Arena Urșilor Junior 2026 appeared first on Stiri pozitive .

mix_items.title: High levels of forever chemicals found on wastewater filters
mix_items.summary: As cities across the nation and the globe increasingly turn to advanced water purification systems to expand their drinking water supplies, researchers at Texas A&M University have identified a critical environmental safety consideration. A new study from the Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering shows that per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS), commonly known as "forever chemicals," accumulate significantly on the purification filters used in advanced treatment to recycle wastewater into drinking water.

mix_items.title: Mediterranean diet is associated with better psychological well-being, study suggests
mix_items.summary: Following a Mediterranean diet may provide additional benefits for mental health. A new study has now linked it to greater psychological well-being among people over 50. The research is the result of a collaboration between University College London and the Barcelona Institute for Global Health (ISGlobal), a center supported by the "la Caixa" Foundation, and has been published in BMJ Open.

mix_items.title: Blood test may help identify which colorectal cancer patients most likely to benefit from chemotherapy after surgery
mix_items.summary: A blood test may help identify which patients with colorectal cancer that has spread to the liver are most likely to benefit from chemotherapy after surgery, according to research presented today at the ESMO Gastrointestinal Cancers Congress 2026.

mix_items.title: Did elephant energetics decide Hannibal's Alpine crossing route?
mix_items.summary: A new analysis sheds light on the most likely route for the Carthaginian general's famous crossing of the Alps. The study, led by the University of Oxford and iDiv/Friedrich Schiller University Jena, reveals that the Col de la Traversette would have been the least energy-intensive route. The findings have been published in Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences.

mix_items.title: Cave finds reveal modern humans and Neanderthals may have shared long-term cultural continuity
mix_items.summary: Tens of thousands of years ago, Homo sapiens coexisted with Neanderthals, Homo neanderthalensis. Many of us living today carry a small amount of Neanderthal DNA, indicating that the two species may have shared much more than just the same land. Now, a breakthrough archaeological discovery has revealed that the two species did not merely cross paths: they possibly shared a common culture that spanned more than 20,000 years.

mix_items.title: Physics-informed AI could accelerate development of controlled-release drug patches, bandages
mix_items.summary: Brown University researchers have developed a new artificial intelligence method for predicting the rate at which materials used in controlled drug-release systems release therapeutic agents. The new method could slash the development time for new therapeutic patches, bandages and implants.

mix_items.title: Bees reveal emotion-like reactions, from 'lip licking' to head shaking, in new videos
mix_items.summary: New research proving bumblebees exhibit emotion-like behaviors—previously thought to exist only in mammalian species—has implications for how scientists understand the consciousness of insects.

mix_items.title: Study reveals how the uterine microbiome may impact pregnancy success
mix_items.summary: Researchers from Trinity College Dublin have uncovered new evidence that the communities of bacteria living in the uterus may play an important role in determining whether pregnancy is successful following assisted reproductive technologies (ART) such as IVF. Among the key findings was one notable surprise, which suggests the biological signals that have long been used to govern the timing of embryo transfer may be misleading.

mix_items.title: New brush test detects oral cancer in one hour
mix_items.summary: A paper published in the journal Biomarker Research by a cross-university team led by Queen Mary University of London researchers validates the use of a noninvasive brush biopsy test that can detect oral cancer within one hour.

mix_items.title: AI could hurt employers in race for top talent
mix_items.summary: Companies embracing AI to recruit faster could be damaging their ability to compete for top talent, according to a major new study.

mix_items.title: Neighbors shape plant life more than expected, scientists find
mix_items.summary: Scientists at Leiden University have discovered that plants are strongly influenced by their neighbors, not just above ground but also through hidden networks in the soil. Their findings challenge long-held ideas about how plants shape their environment and could help improve sustainable farming.

mix_items.title: NASA Takes Flight For America’s 250th
mix_items.summary: NASA Administrator Jared Isaacman leads a flyover featuring his personally owned Northrop F-5 Tiger during the Great American State Fair on July 4, 2026, on the National Mall in Washington, D.C. For 250 years, America has pushed the boundaries of what’s possible. From the earliest days of exploration, to the first steps on the Moon […]

mix_items.title: First synthetic protein motor moves along DNA in controlled, programmable steps
mix_items.summary: Researchers from UNSW Sydney have built the first artificial protein motor capable of taking controlled, directional steps along a DNA track. The protein, dubbed Tumbleweed, moves by alternating between three "feet" that bind to specific DNA sequences. By changing the surrounding chemical environment, the researchers can control both when the motor steps and the direction it travels.

mix_items.title: Algorithm determines which firms have advanced capabilities—and what others must build first
mix_items.summary: Understanding how firms build their capabilities, such as managerial skills, technology adoption and innovation capacity, is important for explaining why some firms perform better and how others can improve. However, unlike sales or profits, firm capabilities are not easily quantifiable. So existing studies have used surveys or interviews to assess them, but these methods are costly, time-consuming and susceptible to bias.

mix_items.title: Exposure to everyday chemicals can add up: A toxicologist offers simple steps to reduce your dose
mix_items.summary: Imagine an ordinary Tuesday. You wash your hair, put on deodorant, drink coffee, pack lunch in a plastic container and commute through traffic to get to work. At work, the custodial staff wipes down a shared table with disinfectant. At home, you cook dinner, clean the kitchen and run the dishwasher.

mix_items.title: New AI system provides treatment recommendations for complex blood cancers
mix_items.summary: Researchers from the German Cancer Research Center (DKFZ), the HI-STEM* Stem Cell Institute and Heidelberg University Hospital (UKHD) have developed "HemaGuide," an AI assistant that supports physicians in making difficult treatment decisions. In extensive testing, the system has shown a high degree of agreement with the recommendations of experienced experts and could significantly improve access to specialized and personalized cancer care.

mix_items.title: Four new groups of indigenous cacao varieties discovered in Peru
mix_items.summary: A new genetic analysis of hundreds of cacao trees representing traditional Amazonian varieties grown on farms across Peru has revealed four previously unidentified, genetically distinct groups. Lambert Motilal, with colleagues from the Cocoa Research Center, The University of the West Indies, and Martha S. Calderon and Danilo E. Bustamante, with their colleagues from the Universidad Nacional Toribio Rodríguez de Mendoza, partnered to explore Peru's untapped cacao diversity.

mix_items.title: New virus catalog reveals which pathogens pose the greatest threat
mix_items.summary: In a typical year, scientists discover two or three viruses that have never been seen in people before. The number fluctuates, but the trend has been fairly steady since the 1960s.

mix_items.title: The myth of the 'lizard brain' and the real trade-off inside your mind
mix_items.summary: So many of life's most pivotal decisions come down to one question: Should you listen to your logic or your emotions? Popular culture often frames this tension as a struggle between two minds—a "more evolved" rational layer built atop an ancient "lizard brain" driven by primal instincts. This battle of the brains has also been playing out over the course of evolution, but not as a simple clash between old and new.

mix_items.title: Unexpected discovery yields new graphene oxide production method
mix_items.summary: Researchers in the Texas A&M University J. Mike Walker '66 Department of Mechanical Engineering have developed a new method for producing graphene oxide, a high-value carbon nanomaterial used in batteries, electronics and advanced manufacturing.

mix_items.title: What do people really eat? New global database gives best answer yet
mix_items.summary: A major new resource that provides one of the most comprehensive pictures yet of what people are eating around the world has been introduced in a new study by a UCL and University of Oxford researcher.

mix_items.title: AI test predicts breast cancer recurrence in hours, not weeks, across 3,500 patients
mix_items.summary: In recent years, notable advances have been made in diagnosing and treating breast cancer. However, its recurrence continues to plague thousands, deepening the need to find ways to better predict the likelihood of its return.

mix_items.title: Paleontologists make 'one in a million' discovery of soft tissue preserved in 450-million-year-old fossil
mix_items.summary: Before the oldest dinosaur, before animals or even plants had expanded onto dry land, ancient relatives of starfish called crinoids, resembling stalked sea flowers, were among the first creatures to flourish in Earth's earliest coral reefs more than 450 million years ago. The study of fossilized crinoids helps scientists understand how these animals evolved and offers rare insight into the origins of complex life.

mix_items.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
mix_items.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

mix_items.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
mix_items.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

mix_items.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
mix_items.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

mix_items.title: A surprising brain discovery is forcing scientists to rethink movement disorders
mix_items.summary: A surprising discovery is overturning a long-held assumption about how the brain’s movement center works. Researchers found that two key cerebellar cell types—thought to be tightly linked—often don’t behave in predictable ways, even though one directly influences the other. The finding suggests scientists may have been relying on the wrong signals when studying disorders such as dystonia, ataxia, and tremor.

mix_items.title: Modern neuroscience is rediscovering an idea Freud had 130 years ago
mix_items.summary: What if Sigmund Freud was onto something that modern neuroscience is only now beginning to explain? A new paper argues that today's leading theory of the brain—as a prediction machine constantly anticipating the world—closely mirrors ideas psychoanalysis has explored for more than a century.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover a surprising link between vitamin C and brain health
mix_items.summary: Could something as simple as vitamin C help support a healthier aging brain? In a study of more than 2,000 older adults in Japan, researchers found that people with lower vitamin C levels in their blood also tended to have less gray matter and weaker connections in a key brain network involved in memory, attention, and other cognitive functions.

mix_items.title: Melanoma's secret to cheating death has finally been revealed
mix_items.summary: Scientists have solved a long-standing mystery by discovering the missing genetic ingredient that helps melanoma cells become effectively immortal. The breakthrough could open the door to new treatments aimed at disrupting one of cancer's most important survival strategies.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover a completely different way to fight viruses
mix_items.summary: Researchers have uncovered an unexpected antiviral defense system in sea anemones that works very differently from the one humans use. The discovery suggests evolution developed multiple ways to combat viruses, challenging long-held ideas about how animal immune systems evolved.

mix_items.title: One injection reversed osteoarthritis in weeks
mix_items.summary: A Colorado research team has created experimental osteoarthritis treatments that appear to regenerate damaged joints rather than just relieve pain. In animal studies, a single injection restored arthritic joints to a healthy state within weeks, while a second therapy repaired cartilage and bone defects by harnessing the body’s own healing cells.

mix_items.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
mix_items.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

mix_items.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
mix_items.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

mix_items.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
mix_items.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

mix_items.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
mix_items.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

mix_items.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
mix_items.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

joke_ro.text: Sunt intr-o relatie serioasa cu butonul de snooze.

satire_ro.title: Un procuror DIICOT a stârnit râsul colegilor după ce a pus mâna din greşeală pe dosarul Nordis
